PLAINTIFF ROBERT BENDER'S, AN INDIVIDUAL, Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Portions Of Complaint.

The motion to seal portions of the complaint is denied without prejudice. Plaintiff contends that the seal is necessary to protect the privacy of nonparties. But the sealed material does not merely protect the privacy of nonparties, who are not named or described at any identifying level of detail; it also includes allegations against named defendants that form a basis for plaintiff's invocation of remedies. The court cannot find that the redactions are narrowly tailored.

Defendant does not oppose sealing and raises the further issue that the allegations are inflammatory and may result in prejudice. The court does not see the same risks here of prejudice to the fairness of proceedings as were apparent in People v. Jackson (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 1009, on which defendant relies. (See id., at p. 1025 ["The details of the crimes alleged are highly prejudicial. This, combined with the celebrity of the defendant, has created a torrent of pretrial publicity. Experience teaches that any new development in this case, real or imaginary, leads to intense media scrutiny. News sources from around the world-newspapers, magazines, radio, television and the Internet-are saturated with information (or disinformation) about the case. Unless one is a hermit, such information is hard to avoid. It is unlikely that potential jurors would not be influenced by exposure to the information NBC seeks."].) The basis for a demand for public relief in our court system is not generally private, and embarrassment alone is not generally found to override the public right of access to court documents. (Id., at p. 1024.)

The court will maintain the conditional seal for 45 days to enable either party to make a narrower request for relief.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Off Calendar Feb-17-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ROBERT BENDER'S, AN INDIVIDUAL, MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER to COMPLAINT.

Plaintiff's motion to strike the term "extortionate" from defendant's answer is denied. Accepting the facts in the answer as true, the use of the term plaintiff objects to is not false or improper and is not an instance of sham pleading.

The court denies the request for judicial notice of the article attached as Exhibit B to the Jung Declaration because it is immaterial to the court's resolution of this motion. That a case has received publicity or that a defendant has made a comment to the press concerning allegations does not make the pleading a sham. The court disregards the facts stated in the Bender Declaration because it must accept the answer's allegations as true on this motion.

Proposed order submitted.
